recommended_tutor_guide.objective: Practice proving a project still runs after changes.
recommended_tutor_guide.what_to_build: Add a tiny tests folder to a project and create at least one test that proves the app imports or starts.
recommended_tutor_guide.why_this_matters: As projects grow, tests help you avoid breaking old features when adding new ones.
recommended_tutor_guide.plain_definition: Testing proves that code still works after changes. A test is a small automatic check with an expected result.
recommended_tutor_guide.real_world_analogy: A test is like checking if a light switch still turns on the light after you repair wiring.
mental_model: Fail = behavior changed or bug exists

steps.title: Create tests folder
steps.why: The folder keeps all files for this quest together so you do not lose track of what belongs to this project.
steps.expected_result: You should see a new folder with the requested name.
steps.proof_hint: Take a screenshot or write one sentence explaining how you know this step is complete.
steps.beginner_tip: Do this step slowly. The goal is understanding, not speed.

steps.title: Create test_smoke.py
steps.why: The file gives your project a place to store the code or notes for this part of the quest.
steps.expected_result: You should be able to point to a visible result or explain what changed.
steps.proof_hint: Keep the file in your project folder and include it in your uploaded proof.
steps.beginner_tip: Do this step slowly. The goal is understanding, not speed.

steps.title: Import your app module
steps.why: This step is a small piece of the final proof. Completing it reduces the quest into something manageable.
steps.expected_result: You should be able to point to a visible result or explain what changed.
steps.proof_hint: Take a screenshot or write one sentence explaining how you know this step is complete.
steps.beginner_tip: Do this step slowly. The goal is understanding, not speed.

steps.title: Write one assert
steps.why: This step is a small piece of the final proof. Completing it reduces the quest into something manageable.
steps.expected_result: You should be able to point to a visible result or explain what changed.
steps.proof_hint: Write the answer in README.md or in your submission reflection.
steps.beginner_tip: Do this step slowly. The goal is understanding, not speed.

steps.title: Install pytest if needed
steps.why: This step is a small piece of the final proof. Completing it reduces the quest into something manageable.
steps.expected_result: You should be able to point to a visible result or explain what changed.
steps.proof_hint: Take a screenshot or write one sentence explaining how you know this step is complete.
steps.beginner_tip: Do this step slowly. The goal is understanding, not speed.

steps.title: Run pytest
steps.why: Running the file proves that the computer can execute your work, not just that the code exists.
steps.expected_result: You should see terminal output, a created file, or no errors.
steps.proof_hint: Copy the terminal output or take a screenshot.
steps.beginner_tip: Make sure your terminal is opened inside the project folder before running commands.

steps.title: Put the command in README.md
steps.why: A README forces you to explain how the project works, which builds understanding and makes the project reusable.
steps.expected_result: README.md should explain what you built, how to run it, and what it proves.
steps.proof_hint: Take a screenshot or write one sentence explaining how you know this step is complete.
steps.beginner_tip: Do this step slowly. The goal is understanding, not speed.

common_mistakes: Testing too much at once.
Writing a test that does not actually assert anything.
Not running tests after changing code.
Ignoring failing tests instead of reading the error.
reflection_questions: What did your test prove?
What would make it fail?
Why is a small test better than no test?
recommended_tutor_guide.study_rule: Do not only copy the step. Before marking the quest complete, explain what the step did in your own words.

guides.objective: Build a real SQLite CRUD app so database practice creates lasting proof.
guides.what_to_build: A command-line Python app named app.py that creates workouts.db, saves workouts, lists them, updates one, and deletes one.
guides.why_this_matters: Almost every serious app needs to remember information after the program closes. SQLite teaches the basic database skills used later in bigger apps.
guides.plain_definition: SQLite is a small database saved as a file. Your Python program can open the file, create tables, store rows, read rows, update rows, and delete rows.
guides.real_world_analogy: Think of the database file as a filing cabinet. A table is one drawer. A row is one sheet of paper in that drawer. A column is one label on every sheet, like Exercise, Sets, or Reps.
mental_model: Column = one detail about each item
Query = instruction you give the database

common_mistakes: Forgetting conn.commit(), so changes do not save.
Creating a table but never inserting data.
Thinking a database is the same thing as a spreadsheet. It is similar, but code talks to it with SQL.
Not checking the database file after running the program.
success_criteria: INSERT, SELECT, UPDATE, and DELETE are used
At least two workouts can be listed after restart
proof_required: Include workouts.db if it was created
Include screenshot or copied terminal output showing rows listed after restart
Answer: what is the difference between a table and a row?
reflection_questions: What is the difference between a database and a table?
What does one row represent in your project?
Why does commit matter?

guides.objective: Prove a Flask project still opens its important pages after changes.
guides.what_to_build: A pytest file named tests/test_routes.py that imports the Flask app and checks at least three routes return HTTP 200 or redirect safely.
guides.why_this_matters: Flask lets your Python code become an app other people can use through a browser.
guides.plain_definition: Flask is a Python tool for making web pages and web endpoints. A route tells Flask what to do when someone visits a URL.
guides.real_world_analogy: Think of Flask as a building. A route is a labeled door. When someone knocks on /hello, Flask checks which function owns that door and sends back the response.
mental_model: Route = rule that listens for that address
Return value = what the browser receives
Template = HTML page Flask fills in

guides.skill: I don't understand what CPU usage means.
guides.objective: Practice one skill with a small, clear build.
guides.what_to_build: A small project that demonstrates the requested skill one time in a visible way.
guides.why_this_matters: Good IT work depends on evidence. You need to know what happened, why it happened, and how to prevent it from happening again.
guides.plain_definition: Troubleshooting means finding what is wrong, proving it with evidence, testing a fix, and documenting the result.
guides.real_world_analogy: It is like being a detective. You do not guess first. You collect clues, test theories, and write down what solved the case.

guides.quest_title: Teach Me Quest: I don't understand what CPU usage means.
guides.skill: I don't understand what CPU usage means.
guides.objective: Understand what CPU usage means and how to check it on Windows.
guides.what_to_build: A simple task that requires you to open Task Manager and observe the CPU usage.
guides.why_this_matters: Good IT work depends on evidence. You need to know what happened, why it happened, and how to prevent it from happening again.
guides.plain_definition: Troubleshooting means finding what is wrong, proving it with evidence, testing a fix, and documenting the result.
guides.real_world_analogy: It is like being a detective. You do not guess first. You collect clues, test theories, and write down what solved the case.

guides.skill: I don't understand Active Directory OUs.
guides.objective: Practice one skill with a small, clear build.
guides.what_to_build: A small project that demonstrates the requested skill one time in a visible way.
guides.why_this_matters: IT support uses Active Directory to manage accounts, computers, permissions, and organization structure.
guides.plain_definition: Active Directory organizes users, computers, groups, and permissions in a Windows domain.
guides.real_world_analogy: Think of Active Directory as a company filing system. OUs are folders. Users and computers are files inside the folders. Policies can be applied to folders.
mental_model: Group Policy = rule applied to users/computers

guides.objective: Practice Python functions, lists, loops, and simple output with an exact beginner build.
guides.what_to_build: A command-line app that stores three expenses in a list and prints the total cost.
guides.why_this_matters: Python is one of the best languages for building practical tools quickly, especially for IT automation and AI/data projects.
guides.plain_definition: Python lets you give the computer step-by-step instructions. It is useful for automation, data, web backends, scripts, and tools.
guides.real_world_analogy: A Python script is like a recipe. Variables are ingredients, functions are reusable recipe steps, and running the file is like cooking the recipe.
mental_model: File I/O = reading or writing files

guides.objective: Practice saving, reading, updating, and deleting records in SQLite.
guides.what_to_build: A small Python app that stores workouts with exercise name, sets, reps, and date.
guides.why_this_matters: Almost every serious app needs to remember information after the program closes. SQLite teaches the basic database skills used later in bigger apps.
guides.plain_definition: SQLite is a small database saved as a file. Your Python program can open the file, create tables, store rows, read rows, update rows, and delete rows.
guides.real_world_analogy: Think of the database file as a filing cabinet. A table is one drawer. A row is one sheet of paper in that drawer. A column is one label on every sheet, like Exercise, Sets, or Reps.
mental_model: Column = one detail about each item
Query = instruction you give the database

common_mistakes: Forgetting conn.commit(), so changes do not save.

guides.why_this_matters: Python is one of the best languages for building practical tools quickly, especially for IT automation and AI/data projects.
guides.plain_definition: Python lets you give the computer step-by-step instructions. It is useful for automation, data, web backends, scripts, and tools.
guides.real_world_analogy: A Python script is like a recipe. Variables are ingredients, functions are reusable recipe steps, and running the file is like cooking the recipe.
mental_model: File I/O = reading or writing files

steps.title: Create a folder named python_expense_totaler
steps.why: The folder keeps all files for this quest together so you do not lose track of what belongs to this project.
steps.expected_result: You should see a new folder with the requested name.
steps.proof_hint: Take a screenshot or write one sentence explaining how you know this step is complete.
steps.beginner_tip: Do this step slowly. The goal is understanding, not speed.

common_mistakes: Not saving the file before running it.
Running the command from the wrong folder.
Misspelling variable names.
Confusing strings and numbers.
success_criteria: At least three expenses are added
reflection_questions: What is a variable?
Why would you use a function?
What does the terminal output prove?
guides.study_rule: Do not only copy the step. Before marking the quest complete, explain what the step did in your own words.

guides.objective: Practice one skill with a small, clear build.
guides.what_to_build: A small project that demonstrates the requested skill one time in a visible way.
guides.why_this_matters: A project that works but looks confusing is hard to use. HTML/CSS makes your software readable and usable.
guides.plain_definition: HTML creates the structure of a page. CSS controls how that structure looks, moves, spaces out, and responds to screen size.
guides.real_world_analogy: HTML is the skeleton of a house. CSS is the paint, furniture, spacing, lighting, and room layout.

common_mistakes: Styling the wrong class name.
Forgetting the dot before a CSS class selector.
Using fixed widths that break on small screens.
Not checking the page at multiple screen sizes.
success_criteria: The target skill appears in the code
The project can be explained in README
reflection_questions: What is the difference between margin and padding?
Why use a class instead of styling every element separately?
What makes a layout responsive?
guides.study_rule: Do not only copy the step. Before marking the quest complete, explain what the step did in your own words.

rule: Every quest should teach what to do, why it matters, how to verify it, and how to explain it back.
